source.title: Droplet-based microfluidic platform for high-throughput screening of Streptomyces

gist_memory.paper_problem_memory: Traditional Streptomyces engineering relies on random mutagenesis and low-throughput screening (e.g., microtiter plates). Flow cytometry-based screening can only analyze spores or protoplasts, which do not represent the filamentous, stationary-phase mycelium used in industrial fermentation. Droplet microfluidics could enable high-throughput screening of mycelium and secreted products, but a platform for Streptomyces has not been established.

section_gists.heading: Droplet-based microfluidic platform for high-throughput screening of Streptomyces
section_gists.gist: Streptomyces are key industrial producers of drugs and enzymes. Current screening methods are limited: microtiter plates are low-throughput; flow cytometry cannot screen mycelium or secreted products, and spores/protoplasts are poor proxies for industrial conditions. Droplet microfluidics can encapsulate single cells, support growth to stationary phase, and detect intra/extracellular signals. This study aims to develop a droplet-based platform for high-throughput screening of Streptomyces mycelium, achieving enrichment ratio up to 334.2, characterizing promoters, and isolating hyper-cellulase producers.
process_signals: Problem: flow cytometry only screens spores/protoplasts, not mycelium; low survival of protoplasts; spores too small (≤1 µm).
Hypothesis: droplet microfluidics can overcome these limitations by enabling long-term cultivation and detection of secreted products.
Goal: establish a droplet-based microfluidic screening platform for Streptomyces.

section_gists.heading: Results
section_gists.gist:
Optimized spore preparation: eight-layered filter paper best for monodispersed spores. Spores germinated and grew similarly in droplets vs. suspension, filling droplets by 24 h. Droplet generation optimized: 1000 µL/h oil, 650 µL/h aqueous gave 92-µm droplets at 443 Hz. Fluorescence from eGFP-expressing strain detectable from 12 h, stable until day 3, then weakened; mycelium autolyzed by day 7. FADS enrichment of positive strain from mixed library achieved enrichment ratio up to 334.2. Characterized native and heterologous promoters in droplets; results correlated with plate-based assays. Screened two synthetic promoter libraries (ermE* and gapdh variants) by FADS, isolating variants with desired strengths. Reconstructed strains confirmed activity. For cellulase screening, used lactose medium to induce cellulase expression; fluorescence signal from substrate cleavage detected. Sorted top 0.1% droplets from ARTP mutant library; six mutants showed 69.2–111.4% higher cellulase activity th
...[clipped]...
process_signals: Initial optimization: spore filtration, droplet size/flow rates.
Observation: spore germination and growth in droplets comparable to suspension.
Fluorescence timeline: signal detectable at 12 h, peaks at 24 h, lasts until day 7.
FADS validation: enrichment ratio up to 334.2.
Promoter characterization: droplet results correlate with plate assays.
Promoter library screening: isolated variants with desired strengths.
Cellulase screening: lactose medium induced cellulase; sorted top 0.1% droplets; mutants validated in plates.
methods: Spore filtration through eight-layered sterile filter paper.
Droplet generation: flow-focusing microfluidic chip, oil phase (HFE-7500 + surfactant), aqueous phase (R2YE medium + spores).
Fluorescence detection: eGFP, excitation 488 nm, emission 525 nm.
FADS: dielectrophoretic sorting based on fluorescence intensity.
Promoter libraries: degenerate 18-nt sequences between -10 and -35 regions.
Cellulase assay: fluorescence from cleavage of resorufin-β-D-cellobioside.
metrics: Cellulase activity increase: 69.2–111.4% over wild type.
failures_or_limits: Large droplets (>100 µm) difficult to sort due to inertia; small droplets limit growth.
Fluorescence signal weakened after day 3; mycelium autolysis by day 7.
Some promoter variants showed different activity in droplets vs. plates (not detailed).

section_gists.heading: Discussion
section_gists.gist: FADS enables screening of Streptomyces in mycelial form, unlike flow cytometry. Droplets support entire life cycle (up to 7 days), including stationary phase, better simulating fermentation. Soft mycelium does not penetrate droplets, allowing long-term stability. FADS can screen extracellular products (e.g., secreted enzymes, pharmaceuticals). Throughput is ~100× traditional microplates, with lower costs. FADS is useful for characterizing expression elements and engineering promoters. Limitations: only tested on S. lividans 66; may need adaptation for non-sporulating strains or those with different morphology; droplet size limits biomass; sorting throughput could be increased.
process_signals: Advantage over flow cytometry: screens mycelium, not just spores/protoplasts.
Droplets support full life cycle, including secondary metabolism.
Soft mycelium prevents droplet penetration, unlike filamentous fungi.
FADS can screen secreted products.
Throughput advantage over microplates.
Limitations: only one strain tested; non-sporulating strains may be challenging; droplet size limits biomass.
failures_or_limits: Only tested on S. lividans 66; generalizability to other Streptomyces unknown.
Non-sporulating strains may require different encapsulation methods.
Droplet size (92 µm) limits biomass; larger droplets could increase capacity but hinder sorting.
Sorting throughput could be improved by increasing flow rates.

section_gists.heading: Methods
section_gists.gist: Strains: S. lividans 66. Media: R2YE for spore germination and droplet cultivation; lactose medium for cellulase induction. eGFP expression cassettes integrated via pSET152-hyg. Promoter libraries constructed by Golden Gate assembly with degenerate 18-nt sequences. Spores collected from 7-day R2YE plates, filtered through eight-layered lens paper, diluted to 10^6 spores/mL. Microfluidic chips fabricated from PDMS; droplet generation with HFE-7500 oil + 2% surfactant; sorting by dielectrophoresis. Fluorescence measured by PMT. Cellulase activity assayed with resorufin-β-D-cellobioside.
process_signals: Spore preparation: 7-day cultivation, filtration, dilution to 10^6/mL for Poisson loading.
Droplet generation: flow-focusing, oil/aqueous flow rates optimized.
Promoter library construction: Golden Gate assembly, transformation into E. coli, then conjugal transfer to Streptomyces.
Sorting: dielectrophoretic deflection based on fluorescence threshold.
methods: Strain construction: conjugal transfer of integrative plasmids.
Spore filtration: eight-layered sterile lens paper.
Microfluidic device: PDMS chip with flow-focusing junction.
Droplet oil: HFE-7500 with 2% (w/w) PEG-PFPE amphiphilic block copolymer surfactant.
Fluorescence detection: 488 nm laser, 525/50 nm bandpass filter.
Cellulase assay: 10 µM resorufin-β-D-cellobioside in lactose medium.

global_hypotheses_about_storyline: The authors hypothesized that droplet microfluidics could overcome the limitations of flow cytometry by enabling long-term cultivation of Streptomyces mycelium and detection of secreted products.
They expected that single-spore encapsulation and growth in droplets would mimic industrial fermentation conditions, allowing screening of stationary-phase phenotypes.
They believed that FADS could achieve high enrichment and be applied to promoter characterization and enzyme hyperproducer screening.
uncertainties_to_resolve: Whether the platform works for other Streptomyces species or non-sporulating strains.
Scalability of the method for larger libraries or industrial use.
Long-term stability of droplets beyond 7 days and effect on secondary metabolite production.
Correlation between droplet fluorescence and actual product titers in large-scale fermentation.

events.summary: Flow cytometry-based screening methods can only screen spores or protoplasts, which do not represent the filamentous stationary phase Streptomyces used in industrial cultivation.
Droplet-based microfluidic platform can facilitate more relevant, reliable and rapid screening of Streptomyces mycelium.
Optimized elution conditions to obtain monodispersed spore suspension using eight-layered sterile filter papers.
Spores germinated and proliferated at a comparable rate in suspension and droplet conditions, with droplets almost filled with mycelium at 24 h.
Fluorescence signals from eGFP-expressing strain became detectable at 12 h, intensified until 24 h, remained intense until day 3, and weakened thereafter but detectable until day 7.
Promoter strengths in droplets correlated with plate-based assays; rpsL(XC) was strongest, gapdh(EL)p was 69.0% of that.

chunk_events.notes: Chunk covers optimization of spore preparation, droplet generation, fluorescence timeline, FADS validation, and promoter characterization.

events.summary: Characterized native and heterologous promoters in S. lividans 66 using FADS and compared with 24-well plate cultivation.
Reconstructed vectors with selected promoter variants and validated their strength in wild-type S. lividans 66 by FADS and 24-well plate.
Tested cellulase production of wild-type S. lividans 66 in droplets with different carbon sources (R2YE, lactose, xylan) using fluorogenic substrate.
numbers_or_metrics: green fluorescence at 36 h (xylan), 24 h (lactose), 48 h (R2YE)

events.summary: Xylan medium caused droplet shrinkage after 48 h, making it unsuitable for screening.
numbers_or_metrics: droplets began to shrink markedly after 48 h

events.summary: Screened ARTP mutant library of S. lividans 66 in lactose droplets by FADS, sorting top 5% fluorescence droplets, and identified six hyper-cellulase producers.

chunk_events.notes: Chunk contains detailed experimental results on promoter characterization, library screening, and cellulase hyperproducer isolation using FADS.

events.summary: ARTP mutagenesis library of S. lividans 66 spores was constructed and screened for cellulase hyperproducers using FADS.
Sequencing of cellulase-coding genes in hyperproducer mutants revealed no mutations, suggesting other genomic changes.
FADS enables screening of Streptomyces in mycelial form, overcoming flow cytometry limitations.
Droplets support entire life cycle of Streptomyces up to 7 days, simulating fermentation.
Soft Streptomyces mycelium does not penetrate droplets, enabling long-term cultivation unlike filamentous fungi.
FADS can screen extracellular products, unlike flow cytometry.
FADS throughput is ~100× traditional microplate methods with lower costs.
Most secondary metabolites are not fluorogenic, limiting direct FADS screening.

chunk_events.notes: Chunk contains experimental results (cellulase screening), validation of platform advantages, and a limitation regarding non-fluorogenic metabolites.

events.summary: Fluorescence of mycelium from 24-well plate cultures was measured and normalized by biomass dry weight after 48 h cultivation.
ARTP mutagenesis was performed on spore suspension exposed to helium gas flow for 90 s to generate a random library for cellulase screening.
Statistical analysis used Origin 2019, with three biological replicates for 24-well experiments and hundreds of droplets for droplet analysis; data presented as mean ± SD with Student's t-test.

chunk_events.notes: This chunk contains detailed methods for droplet sorting, fluorescence analysis, cellulase assay, ARTP mutagenesis, and statistical analysis, which are core experimental procedures of the platform.

Here we show a droplet-based microfluidic platform to facilitate more relevant, reliable and rapid screening of Streptomyces mycelium

The most important advantage of the FADS-assisted method over the flow cytometry-based high-throughput screening methods of Streptomyces is that it enables the screening of Streptomyces in its mycelial form
Our study showed that droplets can support the entire life cycle of Streptomyces up to 7 days, including germination, vegetative growth, secondary metabolism, and autocytolysis

the soft Streptomyces mycelium combined with the long-term stability of droplets (i.e., several months) means that the FADS-assisted method is suitable for engineering Streptomyces strains for use in longer fermentation.
Another advantage of the FADS-assisted method overflow cytometry-based high-throughput screening methods is its ability to screen extracellular products.
The picoliter-droplet-based microfluidic platform can sort at least 10,000 individual droplets per hour
The throughput of FADS is a hundred times that of traditional microplate-based screening methods and has much lower infrastructure, labor, and consumables costs
Most secondary metabolites are not fluorogenic and therefore cannot be directly screened by the current FADS method.

event_graph.paper_main_problem: Traditional Streptomyces screening methods (microtiter plates, flow cytometry) are low-throughput or cannot screen mycelium and secreted products, limiting industrial strain engineering.

canonical_events.summary: Flow cytometry-based screening can only analyze spores or protoplasts, not filamentous mycelium or secreted products, which are critical for industrial Streptomyces cultivation.
A droplet-based microfluidic platform can enable high-throughput screening of Streptomyces mycelium and secreted products, overcoming flow cytometry limitations.
Optimized spore preparation using eight-layered sterile filter papers to obtain monodispersed spore suspension for droplet encapsulation.
Spores germinated and grew comparably in droplets vs. suspension, with droplets filled with mycelium by 24 h, confirming droplet compatibility.
eGFP fluorescence became detectable at 12 h, peaked at 24 h, remained intense until day 3, then weakened but detectable until day 7, defining the screening window.
FADS enrichment of eGFP-positive strain from mixed library achieved enrichment ratio up to 334.2, validating sorting accuracy.
Promoter strengths measured by FADS in droplets correlated with plate-based assays; rpsL(XC) was strongest, gapdh(EL)p was 69.0% of that.
Screened synthetic promoter libraries (ermE* and gapdh variants) by FADS, isolating variants with desired strengths; reconstructed strains validated in plates.
Tested cellulase production in droplets with different carbon sources; lactose induced fluorescence at 24 h, xylan caused droplet shrinkage after 48 h.
Xylan medium caused droplet shrinkage after 48 h, making it unsuitable for screening; lactose was selected as the carbon source.
Screened ARTP mutant library in lactose droplets by FADS, sorting top 5% fluorescence droplets; identified six hyper-cellulase producers with 69.2–111.4% enhanced activity.
Sequencing of cellulase-coding genes in hyperproducer mutants revealed no mutations, suggesting other genomic changes responsible for enhanced production.
FADS enables screening of Streptomyces in mycelial form, supports entire life cycle up to 7 days, and can screen extracellular products, overcoming flow cytometry limitations.
Most secondary metabolites are not fluorogenic, limiting direct FADS screening; platform only tested on S. lividans 66; non-sporulating strains may require adaptation.

edges.reason: The problem of flow cytometry limitations motivates the hypothesis that droplet microfluidics can overcome them.
The hypothesis drives the design of spore preparation for droplet encapsulation.
Optimized spore preparation allows testing germination and growth in droplets.
Successful growth in droplets enables fluorescence detection timeline experiments.
Fluorescence detection window informs FADS enrichment validation.
Validated FADS sorting allows promoter strength characterization.
Promoter characterization establishes baseline for screening synthetic promoter libraries.
After promoter engineering, the platform is applied to cellulase production testing.
Xylan medium failure leads to selection of lactose for further screening.
Lactose medium selection enables ARTP mutant library screening for cellulase hyperproducers.
Hyperproducer identification leads to sequencing analysis to understand mutations.
Sequencing results contribute to overall validation of the platform's capabilities.
Validation highlights limitations that refine understanding of platform applicability.

must_cover_points: Problem: flow cytometry cannot screen mycelium or secreted products.
Hypothesis: droplet microfluidics can overcome this.
Spore preparation optimization for monodispersed encapsulation.
Growth and fluorescence timeline in droplets.
FADS enrichment validation (enrichment ratio up to 334.2).
Promoter characterization and correlation with plate assays.
Cellulase production testing and xylan failure.
ARTP mutant library screening for hyper-cellulase producers.
Validation of platform advantages over flow cytometry.
Limitations: non-fluorogenic metabolites, single strain tested.
